heroOverview: Myoclonus is the sudden, brief, involuntary contraction of a muscle or group of muscles that produces a shock-like jerk, occurring as an isolated phenomenon or as part of a broader neurological or metabolic condition. It is among the most common movement symptoms in clinical neurology: the hiccup — which is a form of myoclonus — affects virtually everyone, and benign hypnic jerks at sleep onset occur in roughly 60 to 70 percent of the population. Pathological myoclonus, by contrast, ranges from mild post-hypoxic startle to the severe, progressive myoclonic epilepsies, which are rare but disabling. Identifying the anatomical origin — cortical, subcortical, spinal, or peripheral — determines both the underlying cause and the most effective treatment. Most symptomatic forms respond meaningfully to anticonvulsant agents such as valproate, levetiracetam, or clonazepam, though the degree of control varies with the underlying disease.

keyStats.prevalence: Benign forms (hypnic jerks) affect up to 70% of the population; pathological myoclonus affects an estimated 8.6 per 100,000 persons (population-based neurological surveys)
keyStats.demographics: Affects all ages; cortical post-hypoxic myoclonus peaks in adults resuscitated from cardiac arrest; progressive myoclonic epilepsies begin in childhood or adolescence
keyStats.avgAge: Varies widely by cause: essential myoclonus typically presents in the second or third decade; post-hypoxic myoclonus can occur at any adult age
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated tens of millions worldwide experience benign forms; pathological forms are uncommon but a significant source of neurological disability

definition: Myoclonus (ICD-10: G25.3) refers to sudden, brief, involuntary muscle jerks arising from abnormal electrical discharges in the nervous system. Each jerk typically lasts less than 100 milliseconds and may be positive (caused by a burst of muscle activity) or negative (caused by a brief interruption in ongoing muscle tone, as in asterixis or 'flapping tremor'). Myoclonus is classified by anatomical origin: cortical myoclonus originates in the motor cortex and is often synchronous with EEG spikes; subcortical myoclonus arises from the thalamus, brainstem, or basal ganglia; spinal myoclonus arises in the spinal cord; and peripheral myoclonus originates in nerve roots or peripheral nerves. By aetiology, myoclonus is categorised as physiological (normal, such as hypnic jerks or hiccups), essential (hereditary or sporadic, without additional neurological disease), epileptic (part of a seizure disorder), symptomatic or secondary (caused by a metabolic, toxic, inflammatory, or structural condition), or progressive myoclonic epilepsy (rare, genetically determined, severely progressive syndromes). Neurology is the primary specialty involved. Management requires identifying and, where possible, treating the underlying cause, followed by symptomatic anticonvulsant therapy to reduce jerk frequency and functional impairment.

typesClassification.description: Normal, non-pathological jerks including hypnic jerks at sleep onset, exercise-induced jerks, and hiccups; no treatment needed.
Hereditary or sporadic myoclonus without underlying metabolic or structural disease, often alcohol-responsive; typically benign and non-progressive.
Jerks that are a manifestation of epilepsy, including juvenile myoclonic epilepsy and generalised epileptic syndromes; treated with antiepileptic drugs.
Myoclonus caused by an identifiable underlying condition — metabolic, toxic, post-hypoxic, inflammatory, or structural; outcome is linked to treating the primary cause.
A group of rare inherited disorders (Unverricht-Lundborg disease, Lafora disease, neuronal ceroid lipofuscinoses) characterised by severe progressive myoclonus, seizures, and neurodegeneration.

primarySymptoms: Sudden, shock-like muscle jerks lasting less than 100 milliseconds, which may affect a single muscle, a limb, or the whole body.
Jerks that are unpredictable in timing and occur spontaneously or in response to stimuli such as a sudden sound, touch, or movement (action myoclonus).
Action myoclonus specifically triggered by voluntary movement, which can severely impair activities such as writing, picking up objects, or walking.
Stimulus-sensitive myoclonus that amplifies with startling stimuli — a loud noise or unexpected touch — in post-hypoxic or epileptic forms.
Rhythmic or arrhythmic jerking that may be focal (confined to one muscle group), segmental (involving adjacent regions), or generalised (whole-body).
Negative myoclonus — brief lapses of postural tone causing the hand or head to drop unexpectedly — particularly seen in metabolic encephalopathy.
Sleep myoclonus, which consists of sudden body jerks at sleep onset (hypnic jerks) or during light sleep, often associated with a sensation of falling.
Progressive worsening of jerk frequency and amplitude over time in neurodegenerative forms, eventually interfering with standing, walking, and speech.
Associated epileptic seizures in syndromes where myoclonus is part of a broader epilepsy — particularly tonic-clonic seizures in juvenile myoclonic epilepsy.
Cognitive or cerebellar features (ataxia, dementia) developing alongside myoclonus in progressive myoclonic epilepsy syndromes.
earlyWarningSigns: New onset of brief, isolated limb jerks during rest or activity that were not previously present.
Worsening frequency of what the patient assumed were benign hypnic jerks, now occurring during daytime.
A sudden involuntary spilling of objects due to arm jerks that seem out of proportion to fatigue.
Brief episodes of head nodding or falling down without loss of consciousness in an adolescent or young adult, suggesting early epileptic myoclonus.
emergencySigns: Continuous or near-continuous myoclonic jerks (myoclonic status epilepticus), which is a neurological emergency requiring immediate treatment.
Post-cardiac-arrest myoclonus with preserved or uncertain consciousness, indicating possible Lance-Adams syndrome or post-hypoxic injury.
Sudden, severe generalised myoclonus with fever, meningism, or altered consciousness, suggesting encephalitis or neuroinfection.
Myoclonus with rapid cognitive decline in an elderly patient, raising concern for Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease requiring urgent specialist evaluation.

causes.description: Diffuse cortical injury from cardiac arrest or respiratory failure disrupts inhibitory interneurons in the motor cortex, releasing uncontrolled excitatory discharges that produce severe action myoclonus; this is the most common cause of disabling myoclonus in adults surviving resuscitation.

Organ failure (renal, hepatic) produces uremic or hepatic encephalopathy in which accumulated toxins disrupt neuronal membrane potential; classic presentation includes asterixis — negative myoclonus of the outstretched hands — alongside confusion and altered consciousness.

Serotonin syndrome from serotonergic drug combinations, opioid toxicity, and lithium toxicity each produce prominent myoclonus through mechanisms including excessive serotonergic activation, opioid receptor-mediated spinal facilitation, and direct neuronal toxicity.

Myoclonic jerks are an intrinsic seizure type in juvenile myoclonic epilepsy, myoclonic-astatic epilepsy, and several generalised epilepsy syndromes; the underlying mechanism involves hyperexcitable cortical or corticothalamic circuits that generate brief, synchronised, epileptiform discharges.

Progressive myoclonus occurs in corticobasal degeneration, Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease, Alzheimer's disease at advanced stages, and multiple system atrophy; the common mechanism is progressive loss of inhibitory cortical interneurons as the disease destroys cortical or subcortical circuits.

Progressive myoclonic epilepsies including Unverricht-Lundborg disease (CSTB gene), Lafora disease (EPM2A/NHLRC1 genes), and neuronal ceroid lipofuscinoses are caused by single-gene mutations that disrupt lysosomal, proteasomal, or neuronal survival pathways, leading to progressive cortical hyperexcitability.

Focal cortical lesions from stroke, tumour, or encephalitis within the motor cortex or its connections can produce focal stimulus-sensitive myoclonus by destroying local inhibitory networks, generating a persistent focus of cortical excitability.

riskFactors.description: Surviving cardiac arrest with a period of cerebral anoxia is the leading risk factor for post-hypoxic cortical myoclonus; the severity correlates with the duration of anoxia and the extent of cortical injury on MRI.

riskFactors.factor: Chronic renal failure or hepatic failure
riskFactors.description: End-organ failure causes accumulation of uremic or hepatic toxins that impair inhibitory neurotransmission, producing metabolic myoclonus; control of the underlying organ failure reduces myoclonus severity.
Combining SSRIs, SNRIs, triptans, tramadol, or opioids raises the risk of serotonin syndrome, which presents with myoclonus among other features; medication review is an important preventive step.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of myoclonus or epilepsy
riskFactors.description: Essential myoclonus and progressive myoclonic epilepsies are often hereditary; a first-degree relative with myoclonus or early-onset epilepsy increases the pre-test probability of a genetic cause.
Established diagnoses of Parkinson's disease, corticobasal degeneration, or Alzheimer's disease place patients at elevated risk of developing myoclonus as the disease progresses.

Alcohol transiently suppresses myoclonus in essential myoclonus but chronic use and withdrawal can trigger or worsen myoclonic jerks; alcohol dependence is a modifiable risk factor in this population.
Brain injury occurring in childhood or adolescence, when cortical circuits are still maturing, may create lasting hyperexcitable networks that manifest as persistent myoclonus.

affectedDemographics: All age groups: physiological forms are universal; pathological forms occur across the lifespan
Adults resuscitated from cardiac arrest — the highest-risk group for severe post-hypoxic myoclonus
Adolescents and young adults with juvenile myoclonic epilepsy, the most common epileptic myoclonus syndrome
Children with Lennox-Gastaut syndrome, Angelman syndrome, or other developmental epileptic encephalopathies
Elderly patients with neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's or Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease
Patients in intensive care with multi-organ failure or receiving serotonergic drug combinations
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosing myoclonus requires characterising the jerk's origin, distribution, and aetiology, because treatment varies substantially across subtypes. The neurologist takes a detailed history covering onset, whether jerks are spontaneous or stimulus-sensitive, worsening with action, and any family history. The examination classifies distribution (focal, segmental, generalised) and assesses for associated neurological signs — ataxia, dementia, pyramidal signs — that suggest a progressive syndrome. Electroencephalography (EEG) is the most important investigation: a cortical correlate time-locked to the jerk on back-averaged EEG confirms cortical origin, while absent correlation suggests subcortical or spinal origin. Back-averaging of EEG and electromyography (EMG) simultaneously characterises the onset latency of the jerk relative to the discharge. Brain MRI is used to identify structural causes and assess for neurodegenerative change or post-hypoxic injury patterns. Blood tests screen for metabolic causes: urea, creatinine, liver enzymes, blood glucose, thyroid function, vitamin B12, copper, and ceruloplasmin. Genetic testing is indicated when clinical features or family history suggest a progressive myoclonic epilepsy. Cerebrospinal fluid examination is performed when encephalitis or prion disease is suspected. In drug-induced myoclonus, the history of medication changes is often diagnostic without further investigation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies cortical correlates of the jerk to confirm cortical origin and characterise the epileptiform activity, essential for distinguishing cortical from subcortical myoclonus.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Electrodes are placed on the scalp with conductive gel; you rest quietly while brain electrical activity is recorded over 20-40 minutes; no pain or electricity is involved.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Records the timing, duration, and pattern of muscle activation during jerks, providing information about the anatomical level of origin and whether the pattern is positive or negative myoclonus.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Small adhesive electrodes are placed over the muscles of interest; you will be asked to perform specified movements or remain at rest while the recording runs.

diagnosticTests.test: Brain MRI with and without contrast
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies structural causes such as cortical lesions, post-hypoxic cortical injury, or neurodegenerative atrophy patterns that explain the myoclonus.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie in an MRI scanner for 30-45 minutes; there is no radiation; the scanner makes loud intermittent knocking noises; contrast dye may be given through an arm vein.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for treatable metabolic causes — renal failure, hepatic failure, hypoglycaemia, thyroid disease, and nutritional deficiencies — that produce symptomatic myoclonus.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; includes urea, creatinine, liver function tests, blood glucose, thyroid function, B12, and metabolic markers; results typically within a few hours to one day.

diagnosticTests.test: Genetic testing (targeted gene panel or whole exome sequencing)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies pathogenic variants in genes known to cause progressive myoclonic epilepsies when clinical features or family history suggest a genetic aetiology.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood or saliva sample is collected; results take several weeks; genetic counselling is offered alongside testing to explain implications for the patient and relatives.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses for encephalitis (viral, autoimmune), prion disease (14-3-3 protein, real-time quaking-induced conversion assay), or inflammatory causes when clinical features warrant.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A lumbar puncture is performed under local anaesthetic; you lie curled on your side; a thin needle is inserted between vertebrae; a headache is the most common side effect and usually resolves with rest and fluids.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of myoclonus is guided by its anatomical origin and underlying aetiology. Addressing the primary cause is the first priority: correcting metabolic disturbances, stopping or substituting offending medications, or treating an underlying encephalitis or metabolic disorder often produces dramatic improvement or resolution of myoclonus without specific anticonvulsant therapy. When jerks persist despite treatment of the underlying cause, or when the cause is structural or genetic, symptomatic anticonvulsant therapy is used to reduce jerk frequency and amplitude. Valproate is broadly effective across cortical and epileptic forms and is often first-line. Levetiracetam has become a preferred agent in post-hypoxic and essential myoclonus because of its tolerability and efficacy in large observational series. Clonazepam, a benzodiazepine, is particularly effective for cortical myoclonus and can be used as an add-on when single-agent therapy is inadequate, though sedation limits its use in higher doses. Piracetam in high doses has been used for cortical myoclonus, particularly post-hypoxic forms, and is available in Europe. Zonisamide is a useful option in Japan and for cases resistant to other agents. For progressive myoclonic epilepsies, treatment is complex, requires specialist epileptology, and is aimed at slowing deterioration and maintaining function; sodium valproate and levetiracetam form the backbone, while medications that worsen myoclonus — particularly carbamazepine, lamotrigine, phenytoin, and vigabatrin — are avoided. Multidisciplinary support including physiotherapy, occupational therapy, and speech and language therapy is essential in functional rehabilitation, particularly in post-hypoxic myoclonus where quality of life depends heavily on maximising residual motor function.

medicalTreatments.name: Valproate (valproic acid)
medicalTreatments.description: A broad-spectrum anticonvulsant that enhances GABA-mediated inhibition and reduces neuronal firing. First-line for epileptic myoclonus and widely used for cortical and post-hypoxic forms; requires liver function and blood count monitoring.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces myoclonic jerk frequency by 50% or more in approximately 60-70% of epileptic myoclonus patients; response in post-hypoxic myoclonus is variable.

medicalTreatments.name: Levetiracetam
medicalTreatments.description: Acts via the synaptic vesicle protein SV2A, modulating neurotransmitter release. Well tolerated and effective across multiple myoclonus types; mood and irritability side effects can occur.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinically meaningful reduction in myoclonus in roughly 65-75% of post-hypoxic and essential myoclonus cases in retrospective series.

medicalTreatments.name: Clonazepam
medicalTreatments.description: A benzodiazepine that enhances GABA-A receptor function, reducing cortical excitability. Particularly effective for cortical myoclonus; sedation, tolerance, and dependence limit long-term use at higher doses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Add-on reduction in myoclonic severity reported in 50-70% of cortical myoclonus patients; most useful in low doses as an adjunct.

medicalTreatments.name: Piracetam (high dose)
medicalTreatments.description: A nootropic agent with poorly understood mechanism that reduces cortical hyperexcitability at doses of 16-24 g/day; used in cortical and post-hypoxic myoclonus, particularly in European practice.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Studies show 40-60% improvement in myoclonus severity scores for cortical myoclonus, with modest tolerability.

medicalTreatments.name: Zonisamide
medicalTreatments.description: A sodium and calcium channel modulator with anticonvulsant activity; used as an add-on for refractory myoclonus, particularly cortical and progressive forms.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Responsive in approximately 50-60% of patients with myoclonus refractory to first-line agents in open-label series.

alternativeTreatments.name: Physiotherapy and occupational therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Task-specific training and adaptive equipment (weighted utensils, wrist weights) reduce the functional impact of action myoclonus, particularly in post-hypoxic Lance-Adams syndrome, allowing greater independence in daily activities.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary management in specific PME syndromes
alternativeTreatments.description: In Lafora disease and certain mitochondrial disorders, optimising nutrition and avoiding metabolic stressors can modestly slow progression; specialist dietitian input is part of comprehensive management.

alternativeTreatments.name: Deep brain stimulation (DBS) — investigational
alternativeTreatments.description: DBS targeting the thalamus or globus pallidus has been used in small case series for refractory myoclonus, with some benefit; it remains investigational and is not a routine option.

whySeeSpecialist: Myoclonus that is new, worsening, functionally impairing, or associated with other neurological symptoms requires neurological assessment to identify any treatable underlying cause before it causes irreversible harm. A neurologist with movement disorder or epilepsy expertise can perform or interpret the specialised tests — EEG back-averaging, surface EMG — needed to localise the origin, order appropriate genetics or metabolic workup, and prescribe the anticonvulsant agents most suitable for the specific subtype. Early specialist involvement prevents inappropriate prescribing of drugs such as carbamazepine, which worsen certain forms of myoclonus.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a neurologist with subspecialty interest in movement disorders or epilepsy, depending on which feature dominates. For post-hypoxic myoclonus, an epileptologist or movement disorder specialist at a tertiary centre with access to EEG back-averaging and surface EMG is ideal. For suspected progressive myoclonic epilepsy, a specialist centre that offers neurogenetics and metabolic testing is needed. Ask whether the practice can perform or interpret EEG back-averaging, and whether they have experience with high-dose piracetam or antiandrogen equivalents for refractory cases.
preventionStrategies: Prompt and effective resuscitation after cardiac arrest, including targeted temperature management, to minimise the duration and extent of cortical anoxic injury.
Careful review and rationalisation of polypharmacy — particularly serotonergic drug combinations — in patients with organ impairment or advanced age.
Tight management of chronic renal and hepatic disease to prevent metabolic encephalopathy.
Genetic counselling for families affected by progressive myoclonic epilepsies so that at-risk relatives can receive early diagnosis and monitoring.
Avoidance of medications known to worsen myoclonus — particularly carbamazepine, phenytoin, and vigabatrin — in patients with established cortical or epileptic myoclonus.
lifestyleModifications: Identify and avoid personal triggers — fatigue, emotional stress, bright flickering lights — that reliably precipitate or worsen jerks.
Implement regular sleep schedules to reduce sleep deprivation, which significantly worsens myoclonus in epileptic forms such as juvenile myoclonic epilepsy.
Reduce or eliminate alcohol: while essential myoclonus is transiently suppressed by alcohol, dependence and withdrawal can worsen jerks and complicate treatment.
Use adaptive equipment — non-slip mats, weighted cups, adapted cutlery, keyboard aids — to maintain independence in daily activities despite action myoclonus.
Inform the driving licensing authority as required, as myoclonus can impair vehicle control and many jurisdictions require notification.

recommended: Regular balanced meals to maintain stable blood glucose, preventing hypoglycaemia-related worsening of jerks
Adequate dietary B12 and folate, particularly in patients on valproate, which depletes folate
Good hydration to support renal excretion of any metabolic toxins
avoid: Excess alcohol, which worsens withdrawal-related myoclonus and complicates anticonvulsant pharmacokinetics
Fasting or very low-calorie diets, which can induce hypoglycaemia and metabolic stress in susceptible individuals

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate, regular exercise is safe and beneficial for most people with myoclonus: it supports cardiovascular health, reduces anxiety and fatigue, and can improve overall motor function with rehabilitation. For action myoclonus, exercise should be tailored by a physiotherapist to minimise trigger of jerks during movement; hydrotherapy is well tolerated and builds strength without the fall risk of land-based exercise. Avoid intense competitive exercise that induces significant fatigue in epileptic myoclonus syndromes, as fatigue is a known precipitant. Exercise should be undertaken with an appropriate companion or supervision until the degree of control is established.
dailyManagement: Take anticonvulsant medication at the same time each day and never abruptly discontinue, as this may trigger status epilepticus or marked myoclonus rebound.
Keep a symptom diary recording jerk frequency, severity, and identified triggers to guide dose adjustments at clinic review.
Arrange the home environment to minimise fall risk: remove loose rugs, install grab rails, and use non-slip flooring in wet areas.
Attend scheduled neurology reviews consistently; EEG monitoring may be needed periodically to guide treatment changes.
Notify relevant driving and occupational licensing authorities of the diagnosis as required by local regulations.
Seek same-day or emergency medical attention if jerks become continuous, or if a new fever, confusion, or sudden deterioration develops.
prognosis: Prognosis in myoclonus varies enormously by aetiology. Physiological and essential forms are benign: hypnic jerks require no treatment, and essential myoclonus is non-progressive and typically manageable with medication or lifestyle alone. Post-hypoxic myoclonus (Lance-Adams syndrome) is often severe initially but can improve substantially over months to years with treatment; a proportion of patients regain meaningful independence with persistent rehabilitation. Epileptic myoclonus — particularly juvenile myoclonic epilepsy — has an excellent long-term outlook on appropriate antiepileptic drugs, with seizure control achieved in around 80 to 90 percent of patients, though medication is usually lifelong. Symptomatic myoclonus due to metabolic causes resolves when the metabolic disturbance is corrected. Progressive myoclonic epilepsies carry the worst prognosis: most are relentlessly progressive and reduce life expectancy, though the rate of decline varies by specific genetic syndrome. Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease is rapidly fatal and no treatment meaningfully alters its course.
recoveryTimeline: Response to anticonvulsant therapy typically begins within the first one to four weeks of treatment. Dose titration to optimal effect can take several months. Post-hypoxic myoclonus may continue improving for twelve to twenty-four months after injury as cortical reorganisation proceeds. In metabolic myoclonus, correction of the underlying organ failure often resolves jerks within days to weeks. Progressive myoclonic epilepsies do not recover; treatment is aimed at slowing progression rather than achieving remission.
complications: Falls and fractures, particularly in action or generalised myoclonus where limb or trunk jerks destabilise balance during standing and walking.
Aspiration and choking, in severe generalised myoclonus where sudden trunk or neck jerks interrupt swallowing.
Myoclonic status epilepticus — continuous myoclonic activity — which is a neurological emergency associated with high mortality in the post-hypoxic context.
Progressive cognitive and functional decline in progressive myoclonic epilepsy syndromes, leading to loss of independence, wheelchair dependence, and need for full-time care.
Psychosocial consequences: social withdrawal, depression, and anxiety are common in individuals with visible, unpredictable jerks that interfere with employment and relationships.
Anticonvulsant medication adverse effects including hepatotoxicity with valproate, mood disturbance with levetiracetam, and sedation with clonazepam requiring monitoring and dose adjustment.

supportResources.name: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke (NINDS) — Myoclonus
supportResources.description: U.S. government patient information on myoclonus types, causes, diagnosis, and treatment options, maintained by NINDS.

supportResources.name: Epilepsy Society — Myoclonic Seizures
supportResources.description: UK patient information on myoclonic seizures, including juvenile myoclonic epilepsy, with guidance on treatment and living well with the condition.

supportResources.name: Progressive Myoclonic Epilepsy (PME) Network — CURE Epilepsy
supportResources.description: Research and family support resources for the rare progressive myoclonic epilepsies, including information on clinical trials and specialist centres.

sources.title: Marsden CD, Hallett M, Fahn S. The nosology and pathophysiology of myoclonus. Movement Disorders, 1982 (foundational classification paper)
Caviness JN. Myoclonus. Mayo Clinic Proceedings, 1996
Frucht SJ, Sun MX, Fahn S. Myoclonus and related movement disorders. Handbook of Clinical Neurology, 2011
NINDS — Myoclonus information page. National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke, updated 2023
Bhatt MB, Bhatt AB, Bhatt SG. Post-hypoxic myoclonus (Lance-Adams syndrome). UpToDate clinical overview, Wolters Kluwer
International League Against Epilepsy (ILAE) — Classification and definition of epilepsy syndromes with age-related onset. Epilepsia, 2022
Zesiewicz TA, Chari A, Jahan I, Miller AM, Sullivan KL. Overview of essential tremor. Neuropsychiatric Disease and Treatment, 2010 (provides movement disorder drug comparison context)

confusedWithConditions.name: Essential tremor
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Essential tremor produces rhythmic, oscillatory movements of 4-12 Hz that persist during sustained posture or action; myoclonus produces brief isolated jerks under 100 ms with no regular oscillation between jerks.

confusedWithConditions.name: Epilepsy (tonic-clonic seizures)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Generalised tonic-clonic seizures involve sustained tonic stiffening followed by clonic rhythmic jerking with loss of consciousness; myoclonus produces brief isolated muscle jerks, often without loss of consciousness.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dystonia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dystonia causes sustained or repetitive abnormal postures from co-contraction of opposing muscles, whereas myoclonus is a brief, sudden jerk caused by a burst or interruption of muscle activity, not sustained posturing.

confusedWithConditions.name: Parkinson's disease (resting tremor)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: The pill-rolling resting tremor of Parkinson's disease is rhythmic and suppressed by movement; myoclonus is a brief, arrhythmic jerk that may actually be worsened by voluntary movement in action myoclonus forms.

relatedConditions.name: Essential tremor
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are movement disorders involving involuntary movement, and essential tremor is a key differential for myoclonus; they can co-occur in some genetic syndromes.

relatedConditions.name: Epilepsy
relatedConditions.relevance: Epileptic myoclonus is a major subtype of myoclonus; myoclonus and epilepsy share anticonvulsant treatments and often co-occur in the same syndrome.

relatedConditions.name: Drug-induced tremor
relatedConditions.relevance: Drug-induced movement disorders share common offending agents with drug-induced myoclonus and are assessed in parallel during medication review.

relatedConditions.name: Neuropathic pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Peripheral nerve pathology can produce both neuropathic pain and peripheral myoclonus; the two often require evaluation together.

faqs.question: What is myoclonus?
faqs.answer: Myoclonus is a sudden, brief, shock-like involuntary muscle jerk lasting under 100 milliseconds. It can affect a single muscle, a limb, or the whole body and occurs in both normal forms (hiccups, hypnic jerks at sleep onset) and as a symptom of neurological or metabolic disease.

faqs.question: What causes myoclonus?
faqs.answer: Common causes include cardiac arrest causing post-hypoxic brain injury, metabolic disturbances from kidney or liver failure, medication toxicity including serotonin syndrome, epileptic syndromes, and neurodegenerative diseases. A small proportion have essential or genetic myoclonus without another identifiable cause.

faqs.question: Is myoclonus a form of epilepsy?
faqs.answer: Not always. Myoclonus is a symptom, not a single disease. Epileptic myoclonus — where jerks are seizure discharges — is one subtype. Other forms arise from metabolic, post-hypoxic, toxic, or degenerative causes without seizure activity. EEG testing distinguishes epileptic from non-epileptic origin.

faqs.question: What do myoclonic jerks feel like?
faqs.answer: Most people describe a sudden, brief jolt or shock — similar to being startled — that lasts less than a second. In action myoclonus the jerk fires during deliberate movement, causing objects to be dropped or spilled. Some feel them only in specific muscles; others experience whole-body jolts.

faqs.question: Are hypnic jerks the same as myoclonus?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hypnic jerks — the sudden twitch that occurs just as you fall asleep — are a normal physiological form of myoclonus. They affect roughly 60 to 70 percent of the population, require no treatment, and do not indicate neurological disease.

faqs.question: How is myoclonus diagnosed?
faqs.answer: A neurologist uses clinical history, EEG with back-averaging to detect cortical discharges, surface EMG to time the jerks, brain MRI, and blood tests for metabolic causes. Genetic testing is added when a progressive myoclonic epilepsy is suspected.

faqs.question: How is myoclonus treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment starts with correcting any underlying cause. Residual myoclonus is managed with anticonvulsants: valproate and levetiracetam are most commonly used. Clonazepam is effective as an add-on. Physiotherapy and adaptive equipment help people manage action myoclonus in daily life.

faqs.question: What is post-hypoxic myoclonus?
faqs.answer: Post-hypoxic myoclonus, also called Lance-Adams syndrome, develops after the brain is deprived of oxygen — most often after cardiac arrest. It causes severe action-triggered jerks that impair movement and speech. It can improve substantially over months to years with anticonvulsant therapy and rehabilitation.

faqs.question: Does myoclonus get worse over time?
faqs.answer: This depends entirely on the cause. Physiological and essential forms are stable or benign. Post-hypoxic myoclonus often improves with time. Symptomatic forms from metabolic causes resolve with treatment. Progressive myoclonic epilepsies worsen over years and reduce life expectancy.

faqs.question: Can myoclonus be cured?
faqs.answer: When myoclonus is caused by a correctable condition such as metabolic encephalopathy or drug toxicity, treating the cause can produce full resolution. For structural or genetic forms, myoclonus is managed rather than cured, though symptoms can often be substantially reduced with appropriate anticonvulsants.

faqs.question: Is myoclonus dangerous?
faqs.answer: Most forms are not immediately life-threatening but can impair function and quality of life. Myoclonic status epilepticus is a neurological emergency. Severe action myoclonus raises the risk of falls and injury. Progressive forms lead to increasing disability. Any new or worsening myoclonus warrants medical evaluation.

faqs.question: What medications make myoclonus worse?
faqs.answer: Carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine, gabapentin, and vigabatrin can worsen cortical or epileptic myoclonus and are avoided in these forms. Serotonergic drug combinations can trigger myoclonus through serotonin syndrome. A neurologist should review all medications when myoclonus is new or worsening.

faqs.question: Can I drive if I have myoclonus?
faqs.answer: This depends on the severity of the jerks and local regulations. Action myoclonus affecting the limbs can impair vehicle control and may require notification of the driving licensing authority. A neurologist can advise on whether driving is safe and what the legal reporting requirements are in your jurisdiction.

faqs.question: Is juvenile myoclonic epilepsy a type of myoclonus?
faqs.answer: Yes. Juvenile myoclonic epilepsy is the most common epileptic myoclonus syndrome, characterised by jerks predominantly in the morning, tonic-clonic seizures, and absences. It responds well to valproate or levetiracetam in about 80 to 90 percent of patients, though medication is usually needed lifelong.

faqs.question: What are progressive myoclonic epilepsies?
faqs.answer: Progressive myoclonic epilepsies are a group of rare inherited disorders characterised by severe worsening myoclonus, seizures, and neurodegeneration. Examples include Unverricht-Lundborg disease and Lafora disease. They begin in childhood or adolescence and progressively impair function; no curative treatment currently exists.

faqs.question: What is the difference between myoclonus and tremor?
faqs.answer: Tremor is a rhythmic oscillation produced by alternating contractions of opposing muscles at 4 to 12 Hz. Myoclonus is a brief isolated jerk under 100 ms with no regular rhythm between jerks. EEG and EMG recordings can distinguish the two when the clinical examination is uncertain.

faqs.question: Can stress or anxiety cause myoclonus?
faqs.answer: Anxiety and sleep deprivation increase the frequency of hypnic jerks and can worsen myoclonus in epileptic and essential forms. Emotional stress is a recognised trigger for action myoclonus flares. Managing stress through structured sleep, relaxation, and psychological support can be a useful adjunct to medication.

faqs.question: What is negative myoclonus or asterixis?
faqs.answer: Negative myoclonus, also called asterixis or 'flapping tremor', is a brief interruption of sustained muscle tone rather than a burst of activity. The outstretched hands drop suddenly and flap. It is the hallmark of metabolic encephalopathy — especially hepatic and renal failure — and improves when the metabolic disturbance is treated.

faqs.question: How is myoclonus different from tics?
faqs.answer: Tics are semi-voluntary, suppressible movements often preceded by a premonitory urge; they are associated with Tourette syndrome. Myoclonic jerks are involuntary, cannot be suppressed, occur without premonitory sensation, and are caused by pathological neuronal discharges rather than the habit-circuit dysfunction underlying tics.

faqs.question: What lifestyle changes help with myoclonus?
faqs.answer: Maintaining a regular sleep schedule is particularly important in epileptic forms such as juvenile myoclonic epilepsy, where sleep deprivation reliably worsens jerks. Reducing alcohol, managing stress, and avoiding known personal triggers such as flickering lights all complement medication in reducing jerk frequency.

metaDescription: Myoclonus causes sudden involuntary muscle jerks from neurological or metabolic causes. Learn types, EEG diagnosis, and valproate and levetiracetam treatments.
